_metadata.description: Refrigerant reference guide for HVAC field technicians
_metadata.disclaimer: Always verify with manufacturer specifications and local codes

r22_replacement_options.status: R-22 production banned in US since Jan 1, 2020 (EPA Clean Air Act). Only reclaimed/recycled R-22 available.

MO99 (R-438A).oil_compatibility: Compatible with existing MO (mineral oil) and AB (alkylbenzene) — no oil change required in most cases
notes: Most popular R-22 retrofit refrigerant
System must be fully evacuated of R-22 before charging — do NOT mix
May need TXV adjustment due to slightly different pressure-temp curve
Liquid charge only
Suction pressure runs slightly lower than R-22
Discharge pressure runs slightly higher than R-22
Good for AC and medium-temp refrigeration

RS-44b (R-453A).oil_compatibility: Compatible with MO and AB oil — no oil change needed
notes: Closest match to R-22 operating pressures of any replacement
Lowest discharge temperature of R-22 alternatives
Good for compressor longevity
Liquid charge only
Works well in residential AC, heat pumps, and medium-temp refrigeration
TXV may need slight adjustment
System must be evacuated of R-22 before charging

NU-22B (R-422B).oil_compatibility: Compatible with MO and AB oil — no oil change needed
notes: Lower capacity than R-22 — may see slightly reduced cooling
Higher discharge temps than MO99 or RS-44b
Acceptable for residential AC systems
Not recommended for low-temp refrigeration
Liquid charge only
Monitor discharge temp closely — may need to add discharge line thermistor
Less commonly used than MO99/RS-44b due to capacity reduction

R-407C.oil_compatibility: Requires POE oil — must flush mineral oil from system
notes: Requires oil change to POE (polyolester)
Has temperature glide (~8°F) — use midpoint for superheat/subcooling
Pressures very similar to R-22
Good capacity match
More labor-intensive retrofit due to oil change
Must replace filter drier (POE oil is hygroscopic)
Zeotropic blend — liquid charge only

retrofit_procedure_notes: 1. Record existing R-22 operating pressures, superheat, subcooling for comparison
2. Recover all R-22 from system (do NOT vent — EPA violation)
3. Replace filter drier (required for all retrofits)
4. If using R-407C, flush system and change to POE oil
6. Charge replacement refrigerant in liquid phase only
7. Adjust TXV if needed based on superheat readings
8. Label system with new refrigerant type (EPA required)
9. Compare operating pressures to R-22 baseline — slight differences are normal
10. Monitor discharge temp — should not exceed 225°F

r410a_phase_out.status: R-410A is being phased down under the AIM Act (American Innovation and Manufacturing Act of 2020)

timeline.2025: New residential/commercial AC and heat pump equipment must use lower-GWP refrigerants (GWP < 700). R-410A GWP = 2088.
timeline.2025_and_beyond: R-410A still available for servicing existing equipment — no ban on servicing
timeline.2026_current: Manufacturers have largely transitioned new equipment lines to R-454B or R-32
timeline.future: R-410A expected to remain available for service for 15-20+ years as installed base is maintained

key_points: R-410A is NOT banned for servicing existing systems
New equipment manufactured after Jan 1, 2025 must use approved lower-GWP alternatives
R-410A price expected to increase as production decreases
Stock up on R-410A for service work on existing systems
Existing R-410A systems do NOT need to be converted

r454b_transition.operating_pressures: Very similar to R-410A — approximately 3-5% lower
key_differences_from_r410a: A2L classification — mildly flammable, requires updated safety standards
Slightly lower operating pressures than R-410A
Lower discharge temperatures than R-410A (better for compressor life)
Requires R-454B rated equipment — cannot drop into R-410A systems
Leak detectors must be rated for A2L refrigerants
Brazing procedures require nitrogen purge (same as R-410A)
Recovery equipment must be rated for A2L refrigerants
Charge limits apply per ASHRAE 15 and UL/CSA 60335-2-40
technician_requirements: Additional A2L safety training recommended (some jurisdictions require it)
Must use A2L-rated recovery machines and leak detectors
No open flames during service when refrigerant may be present
Follow manufacturer charge limits — do not exceed nameplate charge

notes: Single-component refrigerant (not a blend) — no temperature glide
Used widely in Asia and Europe, growing adoption in US
Higher discharge temperatures than R-410A — monitor closely
About 30% lower charge required than R-410A for equivalent capacity
Requires R-32 specific equipment — cannot be used in R-410A systems
POE oil compatible
A2L safety requirements apply (same as R-454B)
Common in Daikin ductless/mini-split systems

R-22.notes: Standard mineral oil. AB oil is a common upgrade for better return.

R-410A.notes: POE oil is hygroscopic — keep sealed until use. Always replace drier when system is opened.

R-454B.notes: Same POE oil as R-410A. Follow manufacturer specs for viscosity grade.

R-32.notes: POE oil required. Use manufacturer-specified viscosity.

R-407C.notes: Must flush mineral oil when retrofitting from R-22. POE is hygroscopic.

R-134a.notes: HVAC applications use POE. Automotive uses PAG — do not mix PAG and POE.

MO99 (R-438A).notes: Works with existing MO/AB in R-22 retrofits — major advantage, no oil change needed.

RS-44b (R-453A).notes: Compatible with existing oil in R-22 systems — no oil change required.

pressure_comparison_chart.description: Side-by-side pressure comparison at common saturation temperatures (cooling mode)
notes: R-410A operates at approximately 50-60% higher pressures than R-22
R-454B operates at approximately 3-5% lower pressures than R-410A
R-32 operates at approximately 3-5% higher pressures than R-410A
R-407C operates at nearly identical pressures to R-22
R-407C has temperature glide — use midpoint (bubble + dew / 2) for calculations
Always use manufacturer PT chart for exact values — this is a field reference
